tweets.text:
Never before have so many Americans been outside the labor force:

The number of Americans who are not in the labor force rose by +832,000 in June, to 105.8 million, an all-time high.

These are people who are neither employed nor actively looking for work.

This is now 2.2 million above the 2020 pandemic peak, when the global economy was shut down.

So far in 2026, 2.5 million Americans have exited the labor force.

By comparison, 68.7 million Americans were not in the labor force at the beginning of this century.

As a % of the population aged 16+, this figure is up to 38.5%, the highest since the 1970s, excluding the pandemic period.

The US job market is weak under the surface.

tweets.text:
US small businesses are struggling with inflation:

In June, 21% of US small business owners reported inflation as their single most important business problem, the highest reading since October 2024.

This percentage has surged +10 points over the last 12 months.

By comparison, the reading peaked at ~36% in 2022 and at ~21% during the 2008 Financial Crisis.

As a result, the proportion of small firms that raised their average selling prices rose +2 percentage points in June, to 38%, the highest since January 2023.

This marks the 4th consecutive monthly increase and is more than double the long-term average of ~14%.

Price pressures are hitting US small businesses.

tweets.text:
2026 has been a historic year for well-diversified investment portfolios:

The Permanent Portfolio is on track to gain +16% in 2026, its best annual return in 30 years.

This strategy consists of equal weights of US stocks, 10-year Treasuries, commodities, and cash.

This comes as commodities are up +26% year-to-date, equivalent to an annualized gain of +54% while the S&P 500 is up +10%, or +20% annualized.

On the other hand, the 10-year Treasury bond price return is down -4% so far in 2026, equivalent to an annualized decline of -8%.

Commodities are becoming a key driver of portfolio returns.

tweets.text:
The issue is that all of that data still winds up in Bitcoin. Op return isn’t even the cheapest place to put it, it’s just historically the least harmful place to put it (that’s why it exists).

All of this fork discussion is basically a debate about which bucket to put spam into, since there isn’t a proposal on the table to materially reduce spam or materially increase the cost of the cheapest method to spam.

That’s why I didn’t bother to talk about it over the past year. And I’d see tweets regularly tagging me asking why I’m not talking about it, people claiming it’s the biggest thing and I’m not talking about it (it’s not the biggest thing. I had little reason to talk about a fork with low consensus that doesn’t materially reduce spam.) I have people emailing asking me if Bitcoin is going to die if a fork that just mildly re-arranges where spam goes doesn’t pass (because they somehow think it stops spam, and its leading proponents have called spam an emergency in need of a rapid fix).

Then I’m asked about it on a podcast. So I explained that it’s not an emergency, the fork doesn’t materially stop spam, it comes with some trade-offs, bitcoin is fine, it’s always worth exploring ways to optimize bitcoin for the future, etc.

Then I get dozens of replies from fork proponents asking if I’m compromised, why do I like spam, why don’t I want to stop spam, etc. Most of them (not all) have playground-grade ad hominems attached as well. It’s mostly not their fault though; they’ve been whipped into a frenzy by people saying if they don’t pass this fork then bitcoin dies.

I support people trying to fork if they want to. Bitcoin needs market tests once in a while. But if people ask me my opinion about this fork’s technical merits, the marketing around it, or its probability of becoming consensus, I’ll give my opinion.

thread_tweets.text:
I have no firm view on what size op return should be. And my level of concern for its size is low because all that content can still go into bitcoin anyway.

People that are hardcore about this distinction tend to fall into one of two camps in my observation: 1) they care more about intention than outcomes (ie it still winds up in there, but only “unofficially”) or 2) they care a lot about making a government compliant coin (with the issue being that bitcoin is global and national laws frequently change).

thread_tweets.text:
Right, but that degrades any sense of urgency. Filtering and then a fork have often been marketed as “must win or bitcoin fails” when really it’s a debate about how many chunks spam should be in.

People want plenty of things. Covenants, consensus cleanup, quantum-resistant signatures. Some of these have been looking for consensus for years. Welcome to the  club. Smaller consensus op returns are indeed a debatable metric to consider, but one of the defining aspects of Bitcoin is how hard it is to achieve consensus, and that’s mostly a good thing.
thread_tweets.full_text:

People want plenty of things. Covenants, consensus cleanup, quantum-resistant signatures. Some of these have been looking for consensus for years. Welcome to the  club. Smaller consensus op returns are indeed a debatable metric to consider, but one of the defining aspects of Bitcoin is how hard it is to achieve consensus, and that’s mostly a good thing.

thread_tweets.text:
I’ve read the bip.

I’m sure you are aware of the motte-and-bailey fallacy, but for those who are not I’ll explain it here.

It’s a debating style where you assert an aggressive position, and then fall back to a somewhat more defensible position when the aggressive one isn’t working, and purposely conflate the two or switch back and forth when convenient.

Many leaders of this movement, and by association their followers, have been saying that Bitcoin is under major attack by spam, that it is vulnerable to illegal images, that bitcoin will fail without this fork, that nodes will become unwieldy, or that this fork is absolutely going to happen. That in aggregate is the aggressive position, the bailey. And keep in mind this is path dependent; it emerged from the filter movement that spent years trying to stop spam. I know people who have received death threats for not supporting this fork. You should see my inbox or notifications of people talking about how this is the most existential thing in bitcoin, or that it’ll stop spam or csam and preserve bitcoin as money. That’s what many people assert.

And when I say, “well no, it doesn’t do that, I don’t consider this an emergency, and even if it was an emergency I wouldn’t view this as anywhere near being a solution for the emergency, but I’m open to discussions on spam management for the longer run.” Then people respond by saying “look at the bip, it’s so mild, it admits how limited it is for spam, it’s not really about spam, etc” That’s the semi-defensible position to fall back to, the motte.

But then great, it’s not an emergency. In fact, the reason I have not written about the bip on my main timeline or in an article (either for or against), is because I read it and it hasn’t reached my top ten things to focus on. By default I don’t support forks under a year old unless I consider them true emergencies, so that they have time for proper review.

But for those who want to try for a contested fork, I support their right to do so. 👍

thread_tweets.text:
Some proponents of making Bitcoin more programmable said the same thing when I wasn’t in support of their quick fork attempt either. And similarly, it wasn’t that I opposed the tech in principle; it just didn’t meet my threshold for supporting it.

I’m a consensus conservative; it takes a lot to get me to agree to a change. One of my favorite features of Bitcoin is how resistant it is to change, including to some of the changes I’d personally like to see.

But talk aside, I support action. Happy to let the market decide in August. If I wasn’t asked about it on a podcast, I just would’ve carried on saying nothing and let action do its thing.👍

thread_tweets.text:
I run a node.

It has a block size limit. If it did not, I wouldn’t run it.

As for content, I don’t want data that can be assembled into vile images on my node. I also don’t really want transactions by the North Korean government on it either. But alas. I 

If someone proposes a way to exclude vile images from bitcoin consensus, I’m happy to check it out. This current fork still has many ways to get them in there, it’s under a year old, and the implementation that supports it only has one maintainer, and I don’t particularly want to run a one-maintainer node implementation.

One of my favorite features of Bitcoin is how resistant it is to change. If it could change easily, I’d sell it.

Big fan of market forces though. People have a right to try to fork.

If someone proposes a way to exclude vile images from bitcoin consensus, I’m happy to check it out. This current fork still has many ways to get them in there, it’s under a year old, and the implementation that supports it only has one maintainer, and I don’t particularly want to run a one-maintainer node implementation.

One of my favorite features of Bitcoin is how resistant it is to change. If it could change easily, I’d sell it.

Big fan of market forces though. People have a right to try to fork.

tweets.full_text:
The issue is that all of that data still winds up in Bitcoin. Op return isn’t even the cheapest place to put it, it’s just historically the least harmful place to put it (that’s why it exists).

All of this fork discussion is basically a debate about which bucket to put spam into, since there isn’t a proposal on the table to materially reduce spam or materially increase the cost of the cheapest method to spam.

That’s why I didn’t bother to talk about it over the past year. And I’d see tweets regularly tagging me asking why I’m not talking about it, people claiming it’s the biggest thing and I’m not talking about it (it’s not the biggest thing. I had little reason to talk about a fork with low consensus that doesn’t materially reduce spam.) I have people emailing asking me if Bitcoin is going to die if a fork that just mildly re-arranges where spam goes doesn’t pass (because they somehow think it stops spam, and its leading proponents have called spam an emergency in need of a rapid fix).

Then I’m asked about it on a podcast. So I explained that it’s not an emergency, the fork doesn’t materially stop spam, it comes with some trade-offs, bitcoin is fine, it’s always worth exploring ways to optimize bitcoin for the future, etc.

Then I get dozens of replies from fork proponents asking if I’m compromised, why do I like spam, why don’t I want to stop spam, etc. Most of them (not all) have playground-grade ad hominems attached as well. It’s mostly not their fault though; they’ve been whipped into a frenzy by people saying if they don’t pass this fork then bitcoin dies.

I support people trying to fork if they want to. Bitcoin needs market tests once in a while. But if people ask me my opinion about this fork’s technical merits, the marketing around it, or its probability of becoming consensus, I’ll give my opinion.

@eirhavbru @ScarcityMan It’s trivial to break spam into chunks and then re-assemble it.

But I understand your desire for a coin that you view as more government compliant than Bitcoin has been, and invite you to direct your node and hash power toward your preference.

@SullyMichaelvan @ScarcityMan The current version for August? My view is “vanishingly small”. I don’t have a precise number.

If economic node adoption or miner signaling changes materially between then and now, my view could change.

I have no firm view on what size op return should be. And my level of concern for its size is low because all that content can still go into bitcoin anyway.

People that are hardcore about this distinction tend to fall into one of two camps in my observation: 1) they care more about intention than outcomes (ie it still winds up in there, but only “unofficially”) or 2) they care a lot about making a government compliant coin (with the issue being that bitcoin is global and national laws frequently change).

@StackItDeep @eirhavbru @ScarcityMan Contiguous data has to be re-assembled to be human-readable too.

@donothedge @ScarcityMan I don’t assume people have bad intentions. I do think some of the marketing around it has been very misleading.

Technically I’m mostly neutral on it, which is why I don’t bother to post on my actual timeline about it. Would prefer not to make LN Symmetry harder.

Right, but that degrades any sense of urgency. Filtering and then a fork have often been marketed as “must win or bitcoin fails” when really it’s a debate about how many chunks spam should be in.

People want plenty of things. Covenants, consensus cleanup, quantum-resistant signatures. Some of these have been looking for consensus for years. Welcome to the  club. Smaller consensus op returns are indeed a debatable metric to consider, but one of the defining aspects of Bitcoin is how hard it is to achieve consensus, and that’s mostly a good thing.

I’ve read the bip.

I’m sure you are aware of the motte-and-bailey fallacy, but for those who are not I’ll explain it here.

It’s a debating style where you assert an aggressive position, and then fall back to a somewhat more defensible position when the aggressive one isn’t working, and purposely conflate the two or switch back and forth when convenient.

Many leaders of this movement, and by association their followers, have been saying that Bitcoin is under major attack by spam, that it is vulnerable to illegal images, that bitcoin will fail without this fork, that nodes will become unwieldy, or that this fork is absolutely going to happen. That in aggregate is the aggressive position, the bailey. And keep in mind this is path dependent; it emerged from the filter movement that spent years trying to stop spam. I know people who have received death threats for not supporting this fork. You should see my inbox or notifications of people talking about how this is the most existential thing in bitcoin, or that it’ll stop spam or csam and preserve bitcoin as money. That’s what many people assert.

And when I say, “well no, it doesn’t do that, I don’t consider this an emergency, and even if it was an emergency I wouldn’t view this as anywhere near being a solution for the emergency, but I’m open to discussions on spam management for the longer run.” Then people respond by saying “look at the bip, it’s so mild, it admits how limited it is for spam, it’s not really about spam, etc” That’s the semi-defensible position to fall back to, the motte.

But then great, it’s not an emergency. In fact, the reason I have not written about the bip on my main timeline or in an article (either for or against), is because I read it and it hasn’t reached my top ten things to focus on. By default I don’t support forks under a year old unless I consider them true emergencies, so that they have time for proper review.

But for those who want to try for a contested fork, I support their right to do so. 👍

Some proponents of making Bitcoin more programmable said the same thing when I wasn’t in support of their quick fork attempt either. And similarly, it wasn’t that I opposed the tech in principle; it just didn’t meet my threshold for supporting it.

I’m a consensus conservative; it takes a lot to get me to agree to a change. One of my favorite features of Bitcoin is how resistant it is to change, including to some of the changes I’d personally like to see.

But talk aside, I support action. Happy to let the market decide in August. If I wasn’t asked about it on a podcast, I just would’ve carried on saying nothing and let action do its thing.👍

I run a node.

It has a block size limit. If it did not, I wouldn’t run it.

As for content, I don’t want data that can be assembled into vile images on my node. I also don’t really want transactions by the North Korean government on it either. But alas. I 

If someone proposes a way to exclude vile images from bitcoin consensus, I’m happy to check it out. This current fork still has many ways to get them in there, it’s under a year old, and the implementation that supports it only has one maintainer, and I don’t particularly want to run a one-maintainer node implementation.

One of my favorite features of Bitcoin is how resistant it is to change. If it could change easily, I’d sell it.

Big fan of market forces though. People have a right to try to fork.

@ScarcityMan @StackItDeep @SullyMichaelvan I wrote in detail about economic nodes in a 2024 paper I co-authored, over a year before the BIP existed. https://t.co/SPMwgcb8X9

Not everything is about the “current thing” lol.

@senecaleland @bitcoinmohar1 @mattkratter @ScarcityMan Those weren’t consensus changes. Those were changes anyone can make for their own node within current consensus.

tweets.text:
Sorry to hear about the breakup. Best of luck on that.

-I disagree that the amount of bitcoin is the best metric for success. Maybe that’s what you are optimizing for but it’s not what I’m optimizing for. For a non-crypto example, if you could create a hospital and earn $10 million or create an “adult” site and create $20 million, are you objectively more successful by being that second person? I’d apply that same logic to this space. I go out and write science fiction books in my spare time because it makes me happy and readers like it; it’s certainly not the best way to earn bitcoin.

-For Bitcoin dominance, it used to be up against hundreds of coins, then thousands, and now millions. Excluding stablecoins (which are an extension of the much larger dollar), Bitcoin is worth more than all cryptos combined, which is remarkable considering how they multiply. But it’s a free market. If people want bigger blocks or base layer privacy or more programmability, despite some of the drawbacks that come with those features, there are coins for them. And as years go by, their value and adoption metrics can be compared.
